name: Acute pancreatitis
presentation: Severe epigastric pain radiating to the back — onset over hours

Causes (GET SMASHED): Gallstones (most common ~40%), Ethanol (~30%), Trauma, Steroids, Mumps/viral, Autoimmune, Scorpion venom, Hyperlipidaemia/Hypercalcaemia, ERCP, Drugs (azathioprine, thiazides, furosemide, tetracycline, sodium valproate)
signs: Epigastric tenderness ± guarding — severe tenderness without peritonism initially
Cullen's sign: periumbilical bruising (retroperitoneal haemorrhage tracking forward) — rare, severe
Grey-Turner's sign: flank bruising — rare, severe
Low-grade fever; tachycardia and hypotension in severe disease

bedside: Obs: tachycardia, hypotension, hypoxia (SpO2 <95%) — all suggest severe disease
bloods: Serum amylase: >3× ULN (>420 U/L) confirms pancreatitis — but level does NOT correlate with severity
Serum lipase: more sensitive and specific than amylase; longer half-life — useful >24 h after onset
Calcium: hypocalcaemia = severity marker (fat saponification sequesters Ca2+); hypercalcaemia causes pancreatitis
CRP: >150 mg/L at 48h = severe disease; also consider bacterial translocation if very high
AST/ALT: elevated LFTs suggest gallstone aetiology; AST elevation is a Glasgow severity criterion
ABG: PaO2 <8 kPa (SpO2 <95%) = most strongly correlated severity marker — reflects early ARDS risk
imaging: USS abdomen (early): assess for gallstones and biliary dilatation — guides management (ERCP if biliary obstruction); USS cannot reliably visualise pancreas due to bowel gas

Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen: not needed for diagnosis if amylase/lipase >3× ULN + characteristic pain; used to assess complications (necrosis, pseudocyst, abscess) — typically at 48-72 h if not improving
ERCP: therapeutic — urgent if biliary obstruction/cholangitis complicating gallstone pancreatitis

mx: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation: crystalloid (Hartmann's preferred) — cornerstone of treatment
Analgesia: IV opioids (morphine/tramadol) — adequate pain control
NBM initially, early enteral feeding (NG/NJ) in severe disease — parenteral nutrition only if enteral not tolerated
Oxygen: maintain SpO2 >95%; early ITU/HDU referral if hypoxic or haemodynamically unstable
Cholecystectomy: same-admission for gallstone pancreatitis (mild) — prevents recurrence; ERCP ± sphincterotomy if cholangitis present
traps: Amylase level does NOT predict severity — only confirms diagnosis; a mildly raised amylase can accompany severe disease and vice versa
Hypocalcaemia = severity marker; hypercalcaemia = CAUSE — opposite directions, both examinable
Glasgow severity criteria thresholds (at 48h): Age >55, WBC >16, Glucose >10, Urea >16, PaO2 <8, Calcium <2, Albumin <32, LDH >600, AST >200 — mnemonic PANCREAS (or GLASGOW)
CRP >150 mg/L at 48h = severe; the CRP in this question (120) does not meet the threshold — not a marker of severity here
LDH >350 IU/L = severity (patient has 320 — just below threshold); don't be fooled by near-threshold values
CT is NOT needed for initial diagnosis if clinical + biochemical criteria met — reserve for complications assessment at 48-72h
Early USS is mandatory to identify gallstones — aetiology determines further management
